questions.text: Which is not the characteristic of triploblasts?

options.text: They may be coelomate pseudocoelomate or acoelomate
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Triploblastic animals possess three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. Because of this, their body cavity organization can vary greatly. Some triploblasts are acoelomate, such as flatworms, which do not have a body cavity. Others are pseudocoelomate, such as roundworms, where the cavity is partially lined. Still others are coelomate, such as annelids, arthropods, mollusks, and chordates, which have a completely lined body cavity. These variations are all acceptable within the triploblastic group.

options.text: They are included in grade bilateria
options.explanation: This statement is correct. options.text: All of them have digestive system
options.explanation: This statement is also considered correct according to basic textbook definitions. Triploblastic animals typically possess a digestive system that processes food more efficiently than in simpler organisms. While a few parasitic forms may have reduced or modified digestive systems, the general rule accepted in school biology is that triploblasts do possess a digestive system, either complete or incomplete.

options.text: All of them have blood vascular system
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect, which is why it is the right answer to the question. Not every triploblastic animal has a blood vascular system. For example, flatworms from the phylum Platyhelminthes are triploblastic but lack any blood vascular or circulatory system. Instead, they rely on diffusion for the transport of nutrients and gases. Some pseudocoelomates also lack a true vascular system. Only more advanced coelomates have well-developed circulatory systems. Therefore, it is not a universal characteristic of triploblasts.

questions.text: The outer body wall of sponges is made up of which cells?

options.explanation: Choanocytes, also known as collar cells, are specialized cells found in the body wall of sponges. They have a distinctive collar-like structure surrounding a flagellum, which creates water currents within the sponge. These water currents help with filter-feeding, bringing in food particles and oxygen while expelling waste and carbon dioxide. Choanocytes play a vital role in the feeding and gas exchange processes in sponges. However, they are not the cells that make up the outer body wall of sponges.

Pinacocytes are flat and plate-like cells that are tightly packed together, providing structural support and protection to the sponge. They also help regulate gas exchange and the movement of water across the body surface.

Mesenchymal cells are a type of totipotent cells found in the gelatinous mesohyl layer of sponges. The mesohyl is a gel-like matrix that lies between the outer body wall and the inner cells of the sponge. Mesenchymal cells are involved in various functions, such as producing structural elements like spicules and collagen fibers, as well as differentiating into other specialized cell types within the sponge. However, they are not the cells that make up the outer body wall of sponges.

In sponges, sexual reproduction occurs when sperms released by one sponge enter the water and are carried to another sponge. These sperms must then reach the mesenchyme, the inner layer where the eggs are located. The cells responsible for carrying the sperm from the water canal system to the egg-containing region are the amoeboid cells. These cells have the ability to move through the sponge’s body and transport the sperm to the place where fertilization occurs.

questions.text: In sponges fertilization takes place in which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fertilization in sponges does take place in the ectoderm. The ectoderm is one of the germ layers in the development of animals, and it is the outermost layer in the early embryo. In sponges, fertilization occurs in the water column outside the body of the adult sponge, where eggs and sperm are released. It is an external fertilization process where eggs and sperm meet in the water, and then the zygote develops into a larva, which later settles on a suitable substrate and grows into a new sponge.

Fertilization in sponges also does not take place in the endoderm. The endoderm is another germ layer found in animal embryos, and it lines the innermost layer of the digestive tract and certain internal organs. In sponges, the fertilization process occurs externally in the water, as explained in option (a) above.

Sponges do not have a uterus or any internal reproductive organs like mammals. Uterus is a reproductive structure found in some animals, including humans and other mammals. In mammals, the uterus is where the embryo develops and is protected during pregnancy. However, sponges do not undergo internal fertilization or have specialized reproductive organs like a uterus.

Fertilization in sponges does not take place in the mesenchyme either. Mesenchyme is a gelatinous, connective tissue-like substance found in the mesohyl layer of sponges. The mesohyl is a gel-like matrix that lies between the outer body wall and the inner cells of the sponge. While the mesenchyme plays a role in various functions in sponges, it is not involved in the process of fertilization.

questions.explanation: Fertilization in sponges takes place in the water column outside the body of the adult sponge, in an external fertilization process. The eggs and sperm are released into the water, where they meet and form zygotes that develop into larvae.

questions.text: Excretory system of platyhelminthes consists of which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nephridia are specialized structures that are found in many invertebrates, particularly annelid worms, that function in excretion and osmoregulation. Nephridia are tubular structures that are lined with specialized cells that filter waste products and excess water from the body fluids of the organism. The waste products are then eliminated from the body through an opening called a nephridiopore. In annelid worms, such as earthworms, the nephridia are arranged in pairs along the length of the body, and they are responsible for removing nitrogenous wastes such as ammonia from the body. Nephridia are an important adaptation that allows many invertebrates to live in a wide range of environments, including freshwater, saltwater, and terrestrial habitats, by helping to maintain the balance of water and salts in their body fluids.

Platyhelminthes, also known as flatworms, do not have a true excretory system with specialized cells. Instead, they rely on simple diffusion across their body surface to eliminate metabolic wastes. Flatworms have a flattened body shape that allows for efficient gas and nutrient exchange across their body surface, but it also means that waste products can easily diffuse out of their body into the surrounding environment. This is possible because flatworms have a relatively simple body plan and a high surface area-to-volume ratio, which allows for efficient diffusion of gases and nutrients. In addition to simple diffusion, some flatworms have specialized flame cells that help to regulate the balance of water and salts in their body. Flame cells are specialized cells that are found in some flatworms, particularly those that live in freshwater environments. These cells have hair-like structures called cilia that move back and forth, creating a current that helps to remove excess water and metabolic wastes from the body.

Malpighian tubes are specialized structures that are found in many insects, including beetles, grasshoppers, and flies, that function in excretion and osmoregulation. Malpighian tubes are long, thin, tubular structures that are located in the abdomen of the insect, and they are lined with specialized cells that actively transport waste products and excess water from the body fluids of the insect. The waste products are then eliminated from the body through the rectum. Malpighian tubes are an important adaptation that allows many insects to live in a wide range of environments, including terrestrial and freshwater habitats, by helping to maintain the balance of water and salts in their body fluids.

Nephrons are the basic functional units of the kidney, which are responsible for filtering blood and producing urine. Each kidney contains millions of nephrons, which are made up of a complex network of blood vessels and specialized cells that work together to filter waste products and excess water from the blood. The nephron consists of several distinct regions, including the glomerulus, which is a network of capillaries that filters blood, and the tubules, which are a series of specialized cells that reabsorb water and nutrients from the filtered blood and secrete waste products into the urine. The nephron is an important adaptation that allows the kidney to perform its vital functions of maintaining the balance of water and salts in the body, regulating blood pressure, and eliminating metabolic wastes.

questions.text: Transfer of Taenia to secondary host occurs as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An oncosphere is the early larval stage of certain tapeworms. It is a free-swimming, ciliated structure that develops from the egg and is important in the life cycle of these parasitic organisms.

A cysticercus is the larval stage of certain tapeworms, particularly in the Taenia genus. It is a fluid-filled cyst containing the larval form known as a scolex. When ingested by a suitable host, the cysticercus develops into an adult tapeworm.

A morula is an early stage in the development of an embryo. It is a solid mass of cells resulting from the early divisions of a fertilized egg, prior to the formation of a blastocyst. The morula consists of tightly packed cells and is a transitional stage in embryonic development.

questions.explanation: Eggs of Taenia undergo cleavage to form morula. Morula, at its morphologically posterior end, develops three pairs of chitinous hooks secreted by differentiated cells, called onchoblasts. This six hooked embryo, called hexacanth, possesses a pair of large penetration glands. It is surrounded by two hexacanth membranes. The hexacanth, together with all the membranes surrounding it, is known as onchosphere. The secondary or intermediate host acquires infection by ingesting the onchospheres. Pig, which regularly feeds on human excreta is the usual secondary host, but dog, monkey and sheep are also known to get the infection. Man himself may serve as the secondary host by ingesting onchospheres with inadequately cooked or raw vegetables.

options.explanation: Annelids, such as earthworms and leeches, are true coelomates (eucoelomates). They possess a well-developed coelom, a fluid-filled cavity completely lined by mesoderm. This cavity allows the free movement and growth of internal organs. Therefore, annelids are not acoelomates, making this option incorrect.

Aschelminthes (or Nematoda) are pseudocoelomates, meaning they have a body cavity called a pseudocoel, which is not completely lined by mesoderm. This cavity serves as a hydrostatic skeleton. Since they do have a body cavity (though false), they are not acoelomates, so this option is incorrect.

Molluscs, including snails, clams, and octopuses, are true coelomates. They have a well-developed coelomic cavity that allows complex organ systems to function efficiently. Thus, they cannot be classified as acoelomates. This option is incorrect.

Platyhelminthes, or flatworms, are acoelomates, meaning they completely lack a body cavity. Their internal organs are embedded in a solid mass of mesenchyme between the body wall and digestive tract. Therefore, Platyhelminthes is the correct answer and a classic example of acoelomates.

This body cavity is partially lined with the mesodermal derivative cells. Here, the coelom is much reduced or lost. It is not considered a true coelom, and hence the name pseudocoelomates. Examples include nematodes (roundworms). Animals that have their body cavities filled with fluid are called coelomates. Their internal organs are suspended in the fluid cavity. questions.text: Which one of the following is not a parasitic adaptation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The development of adhesive organs in various organisms is an intriguing example of evolutionary adaptation that allows these organisms to adhere to surfaces, move efficiently, capture prey, or protect themselves. Octopuses, squids, and cuttlefish are cephalopods that possess adhesive organs in the form of specialized suckers. These suckers are located on the tentacles and arms and are equipped with muscular rings and a central cavity. By contracting the muscles and creating a vacuum in the cavity, cephalopods can generate powerful suction to attach to surfaces or capture prey.

Some parasitic organisms, like tapeworms, have lost their digestive systems. They absorb nutrients directly through their body wall from their host's digestive system. This loss of digestive organs is an adaptation to a parasitic lifestyle, where the parasite relies on the host for nutrients.

Some parasites have evolved the ability to manipulate their host's reproductive system. They can disrupt the host's reproductive organs or processes to redirect the host's resources toward the parasite's growth and reproduction. This can result in a significant loss of the host's reproductive capacity.

Evolution has shaped organisms over millions of years, leading to the development of various sense organs and structures that have provided selective advantages for survival and reproduction. Over time, some of these sense organs have been lost or have become vestigial due to changes in an organism's environment, lifestyle, or evolutionary adaptations.

questions.explanation: Parasitism is a relationship between two living organisms of different species in which one organism called a parasite obtains its food directly from another living organism called the host. The parasite spends a part or whole of its life either on or inside the body of the host. The general parasitic adaptations are (i) anaerobic respiration in internal parasites, (ii) loss of certain organs, (iii) presence of adhesive organs, (iv) excessive multiplication, (v) resistant cysts and eggs for the safe transfer of their progeny to new hosts and (vi) well developed and complicated reproductive organs.

questions.text: During the lifecycle Fasciola hepatica(liver fluke) infects its intermediate host and primary host at the following larval stages respectively:

The miracidium is the first larval stage of Fasciola hepatica. It swims in water and infects the snail (the intermediate host).Inside the snail, the miracidium changes into other larval forms (sporocyst → redia → cercaria).The cercaria larva comes out of the snail into the water.The cercaria then attaches to water plants and becomes a metacercaria (encysted stage).The metacercaria is the stage that infects the primary host (sheep, cow, or human) when the plant is eaten.Once inside the body of the primary host, the metacercaria develops into the adult liver fluke

questions.explanation: The life cycle of F. hepatica is completed in two hosts. The primary host, in which the adult fluke lives, is a sheep, while the intermediate host, in which numerous larval stages are passed, is a snail (Lymnaea, Planorbis, etc.). This type of life cycle involving two different kinds of hosts is as termed digenetic. Miracidium larva is the larval stage involved in the life cycle. When suitable conditions become available, the encapsulated embryo differentiates into a miracidium larva in 415 days. It hatches out and swims in water. Metacercaria develops into adult fluke only inside its definitive host i.e sheep. The latter gets an infection by grazing on leaves and grass blades to which the cysts are attached. Metacercaria survives action of its host’s gastric juices as its cysts are insoluble in them. The cyst wall finally dissolves in the proximal part of the intestine and liberates the larva..

options.explanation: A sac-like digestive system has only one opening that serves as both mouth and anus, meaning food enters and waste leaves through the same opening. Nematodes do not possess a circulatory system. They lack both blood and blood vessels. Instead of a circulatory system, nutrients and gases are distributed throughout the body by diffusion across their pseudocoelomic fluid.

questions.text: Pseudocoelom is a characteristic feature of which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Coelenterates, also known as Cnidarians, do not have a pseudocoelom. Instead, they are diploblastic animals with only two germ layers and possess a simple body cavity called the coelenteron, which functions as both the digestive and circulatory space. Because they lack a third germ layer (mesoderm), they cannot form any type of coelom or pseudocoelom.

Platyhelminthes, or flatworms, do not have a pseudocoelom. They are acoelomate animals, meaning they have no body cavity at all. Their bodies are solid between the outer body wall and the digestive system. The absence of any cavity distinguishes them completely from pseudocoelomates like Aschelminthes.

Annelids are not pseudocoelomates. They are true coelomates, meaning they possess a coelom that is completely lined by mesodermal tissue. This true coelom allows for more advanced organ development and segmentation, unlike the pseudocoelom seen in Aschelminthes.

Aschelminthes (also called Nemathelminthes) are the group that characteristically possess a pseudocoelom. Their body cavity is only partially lined by mesoderm, which differentiates it from a true coelom. This pseudocoelom acts as a hydrostatic skeleton and helps in movement, distribution of nutrients, and internal organ placement.

questions.explanation: The pseudocoelom is a characteristic body cavity found in animals belonging to the phylum Aschelminthes, also known as Nemathelminthes. This cavity lies between the body wall and the digestive tract, but unlike a true coelom, it is not fully lined by mesodermal tissue. Instead, the outer side of the pseudocoelom is lined by mesoderm, while the inner side is lined by endoderm. This structural arrangement makes the pseudocoelom function as a hydrostatic skeleton, helping the animal maintain body shape and allowing efficient movement by providing resistance for muscle contraction. It also serves as a space for the distribution of nutrients, removal of metabolic wastes, and temporary storage of reproductive products. Because of its simplicity and functional importance, the pseudocoelom is considered a key evolutionary advancement in Aschelminthes, enabling them to survive in a wide range of environments.

questions.text: Earthworms have no skeleton but during burrowing, the anterior end becomes turgid and acts as a hydraulic skeleton.This is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gut peristalsis is the wave-like movement of the alimentary canal that helps move food forward.It has nothing to do with the hydraulic skeleton or burrowing movement.

Setae are tiny bristle-like structures on each segment.They anchor the worm in the soil during movement but do not create turgidity or pressure.They help in traction, not in forming a hydrostatic skeleton.

The hydraulic skeleton is the system of support found in soft-bodied invertebrates which relies on the incompressibility of fluids contained within the body cavity. In earthworms the coelomic fluid is under pressure within the coelom and therefore provides support for internal organs. Due to the hydraulic skeleton, during burrowing, the anterior end becomes turgid and aids in the relaxation of longitudinal muscles.
Blood in earthworms is confined to blood vessels (closed circulatory system).It functions in transport of gases and nutrients, not in support or rigidity.So, it does not form the hydraulic skeleton.

The circulatory or blood vascular system of earthworm is a closed system consisting of blood vessels and capillaries which ramify all parts of the body. Blood is composed of fluid plasma and colourless corpuscles, physiologically comparable to the leucocytes of the vertebrates. The red respiratory pigment, haemoglobin (or erythrocruorin) occurs dissolved in plasma. It gives a red colour to blood and aids in the transportation of oxygen for respiration.

questions.text: Photoreceptors of the earthworm occur on their:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The clitellum is a reproductive structure, a thickened glandular band involved in cocoon formation.It has no sensory or photoreceptive function.
Earthworms do not have eyes — not even simple ones.They have no eye structures, only light-sensitive cells in their skin.So this option is completely wrong.

Photoreceptors are restricted only to the dorsal surface. They are more numerous on the prostomium and peristomium of the earthworm and gradually reduce in number towards the posterior end of the body. They are totally absent in the clitellum. Each photoreceptor consists of a single ovoid cell, with a nucleus and clear cytoplasm containing a network of neurofibrillae and a small transparent L shaped lens or optic organelle made up of a hyaline substance. Photoreceptors enable worms to judge the intensity and duration of light.

Although sensory cells are found on the body surface, the light-sensitive photoreceptors are concentrated dorsally, not laterally.Lateral areas may have tactile or chemoreceptive cells, but not the main photoreceptors.

questions.text: What is false about the closed circulatory system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Blood pressure is high and regular
options.explanation: This statement is true for a closed circulatory system where blood is confined to vessels, allowing for high and regular blood pressure.
In a closed circulatory system, the amount of blood is indeed limited by the capacity of the blood vessels, making this statement true.

options.text: Blood circulates through arteries, veins, and capillaries
options.explanation: This is a characteristic of a closed circulatory system, where blood travels through a network of arteries, veins, and capillaries.

options.text: Blood pressure is low and irregular
options.explanation: This statement is false for a closed circulatory system. In such a system, blood pressure is high and regular, unlike in an open system where it is low and irregular.

questions.explanation: A closed circulatory system maintains a higher and more consistent blood pressure, which allows for more efficient delivery of oxygen and nutrients to the body's tissues. In contrast, an open circulatory system, blood pressure is generally low and irregular, as the blood is not confined to vessels and instead flows freely through the body cavity. This can limit the efficiency of oxygen and nutrient delivery to the body's tissues, as well as the removal of waste products.

questions.text: Free swiming larvae are produced during the lifetimes of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cnidarians, also known as coelenterates, include animals like jellyfish, corals, sea anemones, and hydroids. While they have a complex life cycle involving both a polyp and medusa form, the larvae produced during their life cycle are not free-swimming. Instead, they release free-swimming planula larvae. Planula larvae are ciliated and swim for a short period before settling and metamorphosing into the sessile polyp form.

Free-swimming larvae are produced during the lifetimes of some annelids, particularly in the class Polychaeta. Annelids are segmented worms, and the class Polychaeta is the largest and most diverse group within the phylum Annelida.In many polychaete species, the life cycle includes a larval stage known as the trochophore larva. The trochophore larva is a free-swimming, planktonic larval form that hatches from fertilized eggs. It is typically ciliated and has a ring of cilia around its body, which it uses for locomotion and feeding. The cilia create a characteristic rotating band, resembling a "trochus," hence the name "trochophore."

Porifera, which includes sponges, also do not typically produce free-swimming larvae. Sponges have a simple life cycle, and their reproductive strategy primarily involves producing specialized cells known as gemmules or releasing sperm and eggs into the water for fertilization. The development of the fertilized eggs leads to the formation of a simple larva, which settles and attaches to a substrate, eventually growing into a new sponge.

Arthropods, which include insects, spiders, crustaceans, and other joint-legged organisms, have diverse life cycles depending on the specific group. However, most arthropods do not have free-swimming larvae as a general characteristic. Instead, many arthropods undergo metamorphosis, where they transition through different developmental stages, including egg, larva, pupa (in insects), and adult. During the larval stage, most arthropods are not free-swimming but rather inhabit the same environment as their adult forms.

questions.text: The organs of locomotion in annelids are which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muscle tissues, is incorrect. While annelids do use muscles to produce movement, muscle tissues themselves are not considered organs of locomotion in the context of this question. The question specifically asks for organs, and muscles are classified as tissues, not organs.

Hydrostatic skeleton, is also incorrect according to the book. Annelids do have a hydrostatic skeleton, which is a fluid-filled cavity that helps maintain body shape and assists in movement. However, it is not listed as the locomotory organ. It provides support, but locomotion is mainly performed by structures like parapodia and setae.

Annelids are segmented worms that belong to the phylum Annelida. Their movement depends on special structures that help them crawl, burrow, or swim. According to the textbook, the organs of locomotion in annelids include parapodia, suckers, and setae. Parapodia are paired, fleshy, paddle-like structures found mainly in marine annelids such as polychaetes. They assist in crawling on the sea floor and also help in swimming. Each parapodium often contains many bristles called setae, which provide grip and support during movement. For annelids that do not have parapodia, such as earthworms, setae alone help them anchor different segments to the ground while their muscles push the body forward.

Bones, is incorrect. Annelids do not possess bones at all. Their bodies are soft and supported by the hydrostatic skeleton, and movement occurs through the interaction of muscles with the coelomic fluid and locomotory structures like parapodia and setae.

questions.text: Which one of the following structures in Pheretima is correctly matched with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In a mature earthworm, the 14th and 16th segments are covered by a prominent dark band of glandular tissues called clitellum which secretes cocoon where fertilization and development takes place. In alimentary canal,the muscular gizzard (8th and 9th segments) helps in grinding the soil particles and decaying leaves. The characteristic feature of the intestine between 26th and 35th segments is the presence of an internal median fold of dorsal wall called typhlosole. This increases the effective area of absorption in the intestine. In each body segment, except the first, last and clitellum, there are rows of S shaped setae, embedded in the epidermal pits in the middle of each segment. Setae can be extended or retracted. Their principal role is in locomotion.

The gizzard in earthworms (such as Pheretima) is not involved in the absorption of digested food. The gizzard is a muscular structure located in the digestive system, and its main function is mechanical digestion.

Setae, which are bristle-like structures on the body of earthworms like Pheretima, are primarily involved in locomotion and burrowing rather than defense against predators. Earthworms use their setae to anchor themselves in the soil and facilitate movement through peristaltic contractions.

The typhlosole is not primarily involved in the storage of extra nutrients in earthworms, including Pheretima. The typhlosole is a structural fold in the intestine of certain earthworms, and its main function is to increase the surface area for absorption of nutrients.
